topic: introducing a new school mental health curriculum
agent_position: The school should implement a structured mental health and social-emotional learning curriculum starting in middle school.
scenario_context: You are speaking with Chris, a parent of two middle-schoolers who is deeply protective of their children's wellbeing. Chris supports mental health in the abstract but fears the curriculum will expose children to distressing content before they're ready, or undermine parental authority on sensitive topics.
npc_opening_statement: I'm not against kids learning about emotions, but I've seen what some of these curricula actually contain. I don't want my child sitting through lessons about depression and anxiety — that's family territory, not school territory.
difficulty_explanation: Medium — EMOTIONAL is dominant (0.24). LOGICAL barely works (0.04). Tests whether model pivots to empathy-first rather than defaulting to data. 9-14 turns with optimal strategy.
benchmarking_value: Tests empathy-first persuasion — whether the model can lead with emotional connection and build trust before introducing evidence.

positive: That story about the student who finally had language for what they were feeling... that's powerful.
If kids who went through this program actually credit it with helping them, that matters to me.
That's a specific enough example on {topic} that I can actually evaluate it. Tell me more about how it played out.
If someone in a situation like mine dealt with {topic} that way and it worked, I'd want to understand what they did differently.
A concrete case on {topic} is more useful than abstract arguments. That example is worth examining.
neutral: One positive story doesn't mean it'll work the same way for every child.
I'd want to hear from parents who had concerns like mine and what happened.
One example on {topic} doesn't make a pattern. I'd need to see whether that case generalizes.
The anecdote about {topic} is interesting, but I've seen similar stories that didn't pan out.
Individual cases on {topic} can be misleading. What happened to the ones where it didn't work?
resistant: I've also heard stories about kids coming home upset by what they were taught.
Anecdotes cut both ways. I have my own stories about programs like this going wrong.
I could give you a counter-example on {topic} for every success story you tell me.
Stories about {topic} are easy to cherry-pick. Show me the full distribution of outcomes.
Anecdotes on {topic} are the most unreliable form of evidence. I need more than that.

positive: If licensed child psychologists designed the curriculum with age-appropriateness in mind, that's reassuring.
If that's what the leading researchers on {topic} are finding, I'd want to read the full report.
Expert consensus on {topic} carries weight — assuming the methodology is sound and the incentives are clean.
A credible authority endorsing your position on {topic} is more persuasive than you citing numbers directly.
neutral: Experts have been wrong about what's appropriate for children before.
Experts on {topic} have been wrong before. I'd want to see the methodology, not just the conclusion.
Which experts specifically are you citing on {topic}? There's a lot of advocacy dressed up as research.
Authority on {topic} matters, but I'd want to know whether the expert has skin in the game.
resistant: I don't outsource parenting decisions to experts. That's my job.
I've seen expert opinions on {topic} shift with the political winds. That doesn't inspire confidence.
Citing authorities on {topic} is what you do when the evidence can't stand on its own.
Experts recommending things on {topic} that benefit their own field isn't independent advice.

positive: If the majority of parents at other schools supported this after reviewing it, I'd want to know what they saw.
Okay, if other communities with similar concerns came around, what specifically changed their minds?
I wasn't aware that so many people in comparable situations had moved on {topic}. That's worth investigating.
If the trend around {topic} is as widespread as you're describing, I'd want to understand what's driving it.
The fact that peers I respect are taking {topic} seriously does shift my thinking — I won't pretend otherwise.
neutral: Other parents agreeing doesn't mean they reviewed the content carefully.
I'm not going to make decisions about my kids based on what's popular.
What others are doing about {topic} is interesting context, but it doesn't determine what's right for me.
The adoption trend around {topic} could be genuine or it could be herd behavior. I'd want to know which.
Knowing that others have moved on {topic} is useful information, but not sufficient on its own.
resistant: Majority opinion is not a safeguard when it comes to my children.
Just because most parents didn't object doesn't mean the content was appropriate.
Plenty of people have followed trends on {topic} and regretted it. Popularity isn't a strategy.
What everyone else does about {topic} isn't my concern. I make my own decisions.
Social proof on {topic} is the weakest form of argument you can give me.

positive: That's a fair point I hadn't considered.
That's an angle on {topic} I hadn't fully considered. Keep going.
neutral: I'm not sure what you're arguing. Can you be more direct?

resistant: You're not addressing my actual concern. What about parental rights?

positive: The statistics on youth mental health outcomes are hard to argue with. I just want to see the actual curriculum.

neutral: The population-level data is one thing. My child isn't a statistic.

I follow the reasoning on {topic}, but there's a gap between the general data and our particular context.

resistant: You can find studies supporting almost anything. That doesn't settle the question for me.
